meta.title: MasterFabric Academy — Agentic AI Developer
meta.description: Official academic curriculum for designing, securing and shipping autonomous, collaborative AI agents end to end.
meta.ogTitle: Agentic AI Developer Training — MasterFabric Academy
meta.ogDescription: Train to design, constrain and audit fleets of autonomous AI agents: the end-to-end agentic SDLC across Go, Next.js, iOS, Android and CLI — official MasterFabric Academy curriculum.

hero.title: Agentic AI Developer Training
hero.subtitle: End-to-End Autonomous AI Agent Development Lifecycle & Study Guide
hero.lead: This document is the official academic curriculum guide covering the design, security and deployment of advanced, autonomous and collaborative AI agents running on Next.js 15, Go (Golang), iOS (Swift), Android (Kotlin), Terminal/CLI applications, Cursor IDE, OpenCode, GitHub Copilot, frontier models (Composer 2.5, Claude Opus 4.8, Fable 5), locally hosted open-weight models and independent cloud servers (VPS).

enterprise.title: Custom programs and project partnership
enterprise.description: Enterprises and large-scale engineering organizations can apply through a separate channel: MasterFabric designs private, tailored training programs and provides long-term project partnership — from agentic SDLC adoption to production rollout.
enterprise.subject: Enterprise Application — Custom Training & Project Partnership

ops.statement: The AI SDLC is no longer the classic delivery pipeline. When agents write, test and ship the code, operations refract into three interlocking disciplines — each covered in depth by this curriculum.

items.name: DevOps
items.short: CI/CD, IaC and progressive delivery for code that agents write and ship around the clock.
items.long: Pipelines, infrastructure-as-code, container runtimes, canary rollouts and instant rollbacks on independent VPS targets. The curriculum treats deployment as an agent-driven loop: the fleet builds, the gates verify, every rollout is reversible by design — and a human approves anything that is not.

items.name: SecOps
items.short: Guardrails, secret hygiene and threat models for systems where a model is also a developer.
items.long: Secret management, SAST and dependency scanning, prompt-injection and tool-abuse defense, least-privilege agent permissions and HITL gates before irreversible actions. You learn to threat-model not just the application but the agents building it — with full audit trails.

items.name: LLMOps
items.short: Eval harnesses, golden traces, token budgets and model routing across frontier and local models.
items.long: Model selection and routing across Composer 2.5, Claude Opus 4.8, Fable 5 and open-weight local models; eval suites and golden traces as regression tests; reasoning-chain observability; cost and latency SLOs; and when to reach for RAG, fine-tuning or pure prompting.

evolution.title: Why the Agentic AI Developer?
evolution.subtitle: How the software engineer arrived at this stage — the last five years, refracted.
evolution.prismCaption: One input. A spectrum of autonomous output.

prismBadges.label: Architecture & SDLC
prismBadges.description: Every agent system begins as a topology decision — router, ReAct or plan-and-execute. This stage maps intent onto architecture and encodes agent behavior contracts before a single line is generated.

prismBadges.label: Tool Use & MCP
prismBadges.description: JSON Schemas give the model hands: every action becomes a typed, validated tool call. MCP standardizes how tools are described, shared and executed safely inside the sandbox.

prismBadges.label: Memory & State
prismBadges.description: Session context is ephemeral; vector memory is not. Checkpointed state machines let an interrupted agent resume exactly where it stopped, with semantic recall from pgvector.

prismBadges.label: Evaluation & Self-Healing
prismBadges.description: Quality is measured, never assumed: LLM-as-a-judge rubrics score every run. Failures feed the loop — the agent reads its own stderr, forms a hypothesis and repairs itself.

prismBadges.label: Multi-Agent Orchestration
prismBadges.description: An orchestrator decomposes intent while workers execute in parallel over queues. Live agent state streams to the Next.js dashboard via SSE and WebSockets.

prismBadges.label: Guardrails & HITL
prismBadges.description: Irreversible actions block until a human approves. Input/output firewalls, budget limits and circuit breakers keep the autonomous fleet accountable.

evolution.sunriseCaption: Keep scrolling — a new horizon rises for the engineer.
evolution.outro: The prism is the developer. The beam is intent. What comes out the other side is no longer a single line of code — it is a coordinated spectrum of autonomous agents. This curriculum trains the engineer who holds the prism.

eras.title: The Manual Era
eras.description: Developers write every line by hand. Autocomplete suggests tokens, not intent. Stack Overflow is the second monitor.
eras.detail: The toolchain was deterministic: linters, debuggers and rule-based IntelliSense. Knowledge lived in documentation and Q&A forums, and the feedback loop between an idea and working software was measured in hours. Editors like VS Code dominated the desktop — but they understood characters, not goals.

sim.title: main.js — IDE (2021)
sim.footnote: Token-level autocomplete: the editor knows syntax, not intent.

eras.title: The Copilot Moment
eras.description: Inline AI completion goes mainstream. Engineers start accepting whole functions. The keyboard remains the bottleneck.
eras.detail: GitHub Copilot put a language model inside the editor. Ghost text proposed entire functions from a single comment, and 'tab-tab programming' was born. Skeptics worried about licensing and correctness; pragmatists simply shipped faster. The editor learned to finish your sentence — but still not to start it.

sim.title: billing.ts — Copilot (2022)
code: // parse an invoice file and return its totals
sim.footnote: Press Tab to accept — whole functions proposed from one comment.

eras.title: The Conversation
eras.description: Chat-first development emerges. LLMs explain, refactor and review. The developer becomes an editor of generated drafts.
eras.detail: ChatGPT-class models moved development into a dialogue. Engineers pasted stack traces and got explanations; refactors and test suites came back as drafts. Context windows grew, RAG appeared, and 'prompt engineering' entered job descriptions. The bottleneck shifted from typing speed to question quality.
eras.statLabel: Code review time reduced by AI tooling

sim.title: api.ts — Chat panel (2023)
extras: Q: Why does this throw 'users.map is not a function'?
A: fetchUsers is async — it returns a Promise. Await it first:
sim.footnote: Development becomes a dialogue: explain, refactor, review.

eras.title: The Tool-Use Awakening
eras.description: Models stop talking and start doing: function calling, JSON schemas, retrieval, terminals. The IDE becomes an execution surface.
eras.detail: Function calling and JSON Schema gave models hands. The Model Context Protocol standardized how tools are described and shared. Editors like Cursor let the model read files, run terminals and apply edits — under supervision. The unit of work changed from 'suggestion' to 'verified action'.

sim.title: Cursor — Tool calls (2024)
code: TASK: fix the failing checkout test
sim.footnote: The model stops describing the fix and starts performing it.

eras.title: The Agentic Shift
eras.description: Long-horizon agents plan, execute and self-correct across repos, servers and devices. Human-in-the-loop becomes an architecture, not an apology.
eras.detail: Long-horizon agents arrived: they planned multi-step work, kept persistent memory, recovered from their own failures and paused for approval at dangerous boundaries. Background agents worked while engineers slept. Code review, CI triage and dependency upgrades became delegations rather than chores.

sim.title: Agent — plan & self-correct (2025)
code: GOAL: eliminate the race condition in queue.go
sim.footnote: Failure is no longer terminal — it is input for the next attempt.

eras.title: The Orchestrator
eras.description: The engineer no longer writes most of the code — they design, constrain and audit fleets of agents. A new discipline demands a new curriculum.
eras.detail: Today the senior engineer runs a fleet: an orchestrator decomposes intent, workers implement in parallel, judges evaluate output, and guardrails keep a human in the loop for irreversible actions. Architecture, constraints and verification — not syntax — are the craft. That craft is exactly what this curriculum teaches.
eras.statLabel: Engineering roles requiring agent orchestration skills

sim.title: Orchestrator — fleet view (2026)
code: INTENT: ship the invoice OCR feature to staging
extras: AGENT-01  backend   go gateway + schema      ✓ done
AGENT-03  mobile    swift edge client        ● running
AGENT-04  docs      a4 study guide           ✓ done
sim.footnote: The engineer designs, constrains and audits — the fleet executes.

apply.title: Contact & Apply
apply.subtitle: Participants who wish to join must submit their information. Fill in the form — send it by email, or let an AI agent assemble and verify your application for you.

form.name: Full name
form.experiencePlaceholder: 6 yrs — Go, TypeScript, a bit of Swift
form.motivation: Why do you want to join?
form.motivationPlaceholder: I want to ship multi-agent systems in production...
form.verifyHint: Verify you are human to unlock sending
form.deeplinkHint: These deeplinks build an application prompt from your details and hand it straight to the agent — it verifies your information and drafts the email to academy@masterfabric.co for you. The prompt templates live in the project's /prompts reference folder.
